3 Our tax liability has decreased since the restructuring.
4 We have been working with this firm for five years.
5 The audit has been in progress for three months.
Aula 13 — Pre-class

Making Proposals — Pitching Ideas

Block 3 — Pitching Ideas. I would like to suggest/propose/recommend + noun/gerund/that clause for presenting business proposals, timelines, and ROI to stakeholders.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary

Vocabulary
Pitch • to present an idea persuasively
"Maria Claudia will pitch the expansion plan to the board."
Propose • to formally suggest a plan
"I would like to propose a new partnership with Houston."
Feasible • possible and practical
"The board wants to know if the project is feasible."
Stakeholder • a person with interest in a project
"All stakeholders must approve the proposal."
Timeline • a schedule of events
"The project timeline spans six months."
ROI • return on investment
"The projected ROI is twenty percent."
Scalable • able to grow efficiently
"We need a scalable solution."
Implementation • putting a plan into action
"The implementation phase begins in Q2."
Expressions
I would like to propose...
"I would like to propose a phased implementation for the Miami hub."
What I am suggesting is...
"What I am suggesting is a six-month timeline with clear milestones."
The key benefit is...
"The key benefit is a twenty percent ROI within the first year."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice
Pitch
Propose
Feasible
Stakeholder
Timeline
ROI
Scalable
Implementation

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar
Maria Claudia is pitching a proposal to the board of directors for a new logistics hub in Miami. She would like to propose a phased implementation plan. The timeline spans six months, starting in Q2. She has consulted all key stakeholders, including the Houston and Pittsburgh subsidiaries. The projected ROI is twenty percent within the first year. The board wants to know if the plan is feasible and scalable across other subsidiaries. Maria Claudia recommends starting with a feasibility study before committing to full implementation.
1. What is the projected ROI for the Miami hub?
A Ten percent.
B Twenty percent within the first year.
C Thirty percent over two years.
D Fifteen percent after six months.
2. What does the board want to know about the plan?
A If it is profitable immediately.
B If it is feasible and scalable across other subsidiaries.
C If the Houston team agrees.
D If it can be completed in three months.
3. What does Maria Claudia recommend before full implementation?
A Hiring a new team.
B Closing the Houston subsidiary.
C Starting with a feasibility study.
D Increasing the budget by fifty percent.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar
I would like to suggest / propose / recommend
Structure Formula Example
+ nounI would like to propose + a partnershipI would like to propose a new partnership.
+ gerund (-ing)I would like to suggest + startingI would like to suggest starting with a pilot project.
+ that clauseI would like to recommend + that we reviewI would like to recommend that we review the timeline.
Key differences
Verb Nuance
suggestsofter, more tentative
proposeformal, structured plan
recommendbased on expertise or evidence

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice
"Maria Claudia will the new logistics hub to the board."
"I would like to a partnership with a Miami-based firm."
"The board needs to determine if the expansion is ."
"All have been consulted about the new plan."
"The project includes three major milestones."
"The expected justifies the initial investment."
"We need a model that works across all subsidiaries."
"The phase will begin after board approval."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order
? "The projected ROI is twenty percent within the first year."
? "We recommend starting with a feasibility study."
? "I would like to propose a new logistics hub in Miami."
? "The solution is scalable across all subsidiaries."
? "What I am suggesting is a phased approach over six months."
? "The key benefit is a strong return on investment for all stakeholders."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking
I would like to propose a new logistics hub in Miami.
The projected ROI is twenty percent within the first year.
We need a scalable solution that works across subsidiaries.
The implementation phase begins in Q2 with a six-month timeline.
All stakeholders have been consulted and support the proposal.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. You want to formally present a new idea to the board. What do you say?
A "I want that we do this project."
B "I would like to propose a phased implementation plan."
C "We must doing this project now."
2. The board asks about the expected return. What is the best response?
A "The projected ROI is twenty percent within the first year."
B "The ROI is going to be very good, I think."
C "I am not sure about the numbers yet."
3. You want to suggest a careful approach. What do you say?
A "We should just start and see what happens."
B "I would like to recommend that we begin with a feasibility study."
C "I recommend to start immediately."
4. A colleague asks if the plan can grow. What is the correct response?
A "Yes, it is very big already."
B "Yes, the solution is scalable across all subsidiaries."
C "Yes, we can scale it down if needed."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection
Imagine you are pitching a new project to the board. Describe your proposal using "I would like to propose/suggest/recommend." Include the timeline, ROI, stakeholders, and why it is feasible and scalable.

Survival Card — Lesson 13

1 I would like to propose a phased approach for the Miami hub.
2 The key benefit is a twenty percent return on investment.
3 The project timeline spans six months starting in Q2.
4 We need to confirm that the plan is feasible and scalable.
5 The implementation will begin after stakeholder approval.
Aula 20 -- Pre-class

The Big Review -- Block 2 Integration

Review and integrate everything from Block 2 in one full negotiation: report results with the present perfect, state obligations with shall / must / may, describe numbers with trend language, give advice with should / had better, and compare fairly with whereas / unlike / different from. Key words: milestone, obligation, warranty, revenue, deadline, track record, option, trade-off, rapport, mindset.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Review each key word from Block 2 and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Milestone -- an important stage or achievement in a project
"Closing the third deal was a real milestone this year." (Marco)
Obligation -- a duty or requirement you are bound to fulfill
"Each party has an obligation to deliver on time." (Obrigação)
Warranty -- a written promise to repair or replace a product
"The contract must include a twelve-month warranty." (Garantia)
Revenue -- the total income a company earns from its business
"Revenue has risen sharply this quarter." (Receita / faturamento)
Deadline -- the latest date or time by which something must be done
"We had better confirm the deadline early." (Prazo)
Track record -- a record of past performance and results
"We have built a strong track record together." (Histórico de resultados)
Option -- a choice or course of action that is available
"We should weigh the options before we decide." (Opção)
Trade-off -- a balance where you gain one thing but lose another
"A lower price is a trade-off for a longer contract." (Compensação / troca)
Rapport -- a warm, easy connection and mutual understanding
"A short comment on art helps build rapport." (Sintonia / boa relação)
Mindset -- an established way of thinking and approaching things
"Keep your warm mindset and add American directness." (Mentalidade)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Milestone
Obligation
Warranty
Revenue
Deadline
Track record
Option
Trade-off
Rapport
Mindset

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Block 2 brought five tools together. Before the final negotiation, Maria Claudia reports her track record with the present perfect: "we have closed three deals, and revenue has risen sharply." She states the obligations with modals: "each party shall deliver, the contract must include a warranty, and either side may request a change." She describes the numbers with trend language, and gives advice with had better: "we had better confirm the deadline today." Finally, she compares the two styles fairly: "they are direct, whereas we build rapport first; their urgency is different from ours" -- the preposition is always "from", never "of". With a clear option and a fair trade-off on the table, she is ready to close the deal.

1. Why does the text use "have closed" and "has risen" instead of the simple past?
A The period is still open and the result matters now (present perfect).
B The events happened on a fixed past date.
C They are commands.
2. What follows the modal in "each party shall deliver"?
A "to" + verb ("shall to deliver").
B The base verb, with no "to" ("shall deliver").
C The past participle.
3. Which preposition is correct after "different"?
A "from" (different from ours).
B "of" (different of ours).
C "than" (different than ours).

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Block 2 Toolkit

GRAMMAR

Esta aula revisa as cinco estruturas do Bloco 2 juntas. PRESENT PERFECT (have + particípio) = resultado até agora. MODAIS shall/must/may + verbo base (sem "to") = obrigação e permissão. TREND LANGUAGE (verbo + advérbio, ex.: rose sharply) = descrever números. ADVICE should/ought to/had better = conselho (had better tem consequência). COMPARISON whereas/while + oração, unlike + substantivo, different FROM. / This lesson reviews all five Block 2 structures together. PRESENT PERFECT (have + past participle) = result up to now. MODALS shall/must/may + base verb (no "to") = obligation and permission. TREND LANGUAGE (verb + adverb) = describe numbers. ADVICE should/ought to/had better = recommend; had better has a consequence. COMPARISON whereas/while + clause, unlike + noun, different FROM.

StructureUseExample
Present perfectresult up to now"we have closed three deals"
Modals (shall/must/may)obligation / permission (+ base verb)"each party shall deliver"
Trend languagedescribe numbers"revenue rose sharply"
Advice (should / had better)recommend; consequence"we had better confirm the deadline"
Comparison (whereas / unlike / different from)contrast fairly"they are direct, whereas we build rapport"

Key point / Ponto-chave: com período aberto (this quarter), use o present perfect, não o simple past; modal vem com verbo base (sem "to"); e a preposição é sempre "different from". / With an open period (this quarter), use the present perfect, not the simple past; a modal takes the base verb (no "to"); and the preposition is always "different from".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"We closed three deals this quarter."
"Each party deliver on time."
"Revenue rose this quarter."
"We had confirm the deadline today."
"Their urgency is different ours."
"A lower price is a fair for a longer contract."

Stage 2: Lead the Negotiation in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of leading the negotiation in the correct order.

?You present the numbers with trend language, such as revenue has risen sharply.
?You report your track record with the present perfect: we have closed three deals.
?You compare the two cultures fairly with whereas and unlike, and adapt your approach.
?You state the obligations with modals: each party shall deliver, the contract must include a warranty.
?You give a clear recommendation with should or had better about the deadline.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

We have closed three deals this quarter.
Fechamos três negócios neste trimestre.
Each party shall deliver, and the contract must include a warranty.
Cada parte deverá entregar, e o contrato deve incluir uma garantia.
We had better confirm the deadline today.
É melhor confirmarmos o prazo hoje.
They are direct, whereas we build rapport; their pace is different from ours.
Eles são diretos, enquanto nós criamos sintonia; o ritmo deles é diferente do nosso.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to report a result in a period that is still open. Which is best?
A "We closed three deals this quarter."
B "We have closed three deals this quarter."
C "We are closing three deals this quarter."
You want to state a contractual obligation. Which is best?
A "The contract must include a warranty."
B "The contract must to include a warranty."
C "The contract should included a warranty."
You want to contrast the two negotiating styles. Which is best?
A "Their pace is different of ours."
B "They are direct, whereas we build rapport first."
C "Unlike they are direct, we build rapport first."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Self-assessment of Block 2: in five sentences, report one result with the present perfect, state one obligation with a modal, describe one trend, give one recommendation with should or had better, and compare your style with a US partner's using whereas or different from.

Survival Card -- Lesson 20

1We have closed three deals this quarter.Fechamos três negócios neste trimestre.
2Each party shall deliver, and the contract must include a warranty.Cada parte deverá entregar, e o contrato deve incluir uma garantia.
3Revenue has risen sharply this quarter.A receita subiu acentuadamente neste trimestre.
4We had better confirm the deadline today.É melhor confirmarmos o prazo hoje.
5They are direct, whereas we build rapport; their pace is different from ours.Eles são diretos, enquanto nós criamos sintonia; o ritmo deles é diferente do nosso.
Aula 19 -- Pre-class

Houston, We Have Culture -- US vs Brazil

Compare business and art culture across the US and Brazil. Contrast two ideas with whereas and while (same meaning, whereas is a little more formal), mark a clear difference with unlike + a noun, and soften with similar to and different from -- the preposition is always "from". New vocabulary: punctuality, hierarchy, directness, formality, etiquette, gesture, acquaintance, rapport, custom, mindset.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Punctuality -- the habit of being exactly on time
"American meetings value punctuality; they start at nine sharp." (Pontualidade)
Hierarchy -- the system of ranks and authority in an organization
"A flat hierarchy lets a junior analyst speak up freely." (Hierarquia)
Directness -- the quality of saying what you mean openly and early
"Americans often appreciate directness in a negotiation." (Franqueza / objetividade)
Formality -- the degree of official, polite behavior expected
"Brazilian first meetings often carry more formality." (Formalidade)
Etiquette -- the accepted rules of polite behavior in a culture
"Email etiquette in the US is informal in tone but precise." (Etiqueta / boas maneiras)
Gesture -- a movement of the hands or body that carries meaning
"A firm handshake is a positive gesture in US business." (Gesto)
Acquaintance -- a person you know slightly, not a close friend
"At the trade show I met a useful acquaintance from Houston." (Conhecido / a)
Rapport -- a warm, easy connection and mutual understanding
"A short comment on art helps build rapport quickly." (Sintonia / boa relação)
Custom -- a usual, traditional way of doing something in a culture
"Building the relationship first is a Brazilian custom." (Costume)
Mindset -- an established way of thinking and approaching things
"Keep your warm mindset and add a little US punctuality." (Mentalidade)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Punctuality
Hierarchy
Directness
Formality
Etiquette
Gesture
Acquaintance
Rapport
Custom
Mindset

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Before her trip to Houston, Maria Claudia studies the local custom. US meetings value punctuality and directness: they start exactly on time and state the point early, whereas Brazilian meetings often build rapport first with a little small talk. Unlike a traditional firm, the Houston office has a flat hierarchy, so a junior may openly disagree with a director. The email etiquette is informal in tone but precise, which is different from the more formal Brazilian opening -- notice that the preposition is always "from", never "of" or "than". The American sense of humor is similar to hers, while the sense of personal space is not. Her plan is wise: keep her warm mindset and her custom of building rapport, but adapt to their punctuality and respect their etiquette, from a firm handshake to the right gesture when she disagrees.

1. Which word does the text use to contrast two full ideas (US vs Brazilian meetings)?
A "whereas" (and "while") -- they join two clauses to contrast them.
B "unlike" -- it joins two clauses.
C "similar to" -- it shows a difference.
2. What follows "unlike" in "unlike a traditional firm"?
A A full clause with subject and verb.
B A noun ("a traditional firm").
C An adjective only.
3. Which preposition is correct after "different"?
A "from" (different from ours).
B "of" (different of ours).
C "than" (different than ours).

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Comparing & Contrasting

GRAMMAR

Para comparar duas culturas, use conectores e preposições certas. WHEREAS e WHILE + oração contrastam duas ideias completas (mesmo sentido; whereas é um pouco mais formal). UNLIKE + substantivo marca uma diferença clara e oposta. Para comparações mais suaves, SIMILAR TO + substantivo (parecido) e DIFFERENT FROM + substantivo (diferente). Atenção à preposição: é sempre different FROM, nunca "of" nem "than". / To compare two cultures, use the right connectors and prepositions. WHEREAS and WHILE + clause contrast two full ideas (same meaning; whereas is more formal). UNLIKE + noun marks a clear, opposite difference. For softer comparisons, SIMILAR TO + noun and DIFFERENT FROM + noun. The preposition is always different FROM -- never "of" or "than".

FormUseExample
whereas + clausecontrast two ideas (formal)"we warm up, whereas they start on time"
while + clausesame meaning, softer"they are direct, while we build rapport"
unlike + nouna clear, opposite difference"unlike Brazil, the hierarchy is flat"
similar to + nounsofter: alike"their humor is similar to ours"
different from + nounsofter: not alike (always from)"the etiquette is different from ours"

Key point / Ponto-chave: whereas/while ligam duas orações; unlike vem antes de substantivo. A preposição é sempre "different from": "their food is different of" → "their food is different from ours". / whereas/while join two clauses; unlike takes a noun. The preposition is always "different from", never "of" or "than".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word, connector, or preposition. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"US meetings start on time, ours warm up first."
" a traditional firm, their hierarchy is flat."
"Their taste in art is similar ours."
"Their email etiquette is different ours."
"A short comment on art helps build ."
"I will keep my warm and add a little US punctuality."

Stage 2: Compare Cultures in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of comparing cultures fairly in the correct order.

?You mark a strong contrast with unlike, when one culture does the opposite of the other.
?You observe the local custom before you judge it, so you compare fairly.
?You adapt your own mindset where it helps, while you keep your own strengths, such as warmth.
?You name one clear difference using whereas or while, such as punctuality.
?You soften the comparison with similar to and different from, because most differences are partial.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

US meetings start on time, whereas ours warm up first.
Reuniões nos EUA começam no horário, enquanto as nossas se aquecem primeiro.
Unlike a traditional firm, their hierarchy is flat.
Diferente de uma empresa tradicional, a hierarquia deles é horizontal.
Their email etiquette is different from ours.
A etiqueta de e-mail deles é diferente da nossa.
I keep my custom of building rapport, and I respect their etiquette.
Mantenho meu costume de criar sintonia e respeito a etiqueta deles.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to contrast two full ideas about meetings. Which is best?
A "Unlike they start on time, we warm up first."
B "They start on time, whereas we warm up first."
C "Different from they start on time, we warm up."
You want to say their etiquette is not the same as ours. Which is best?
A "Their etiquette is different from ours."
B "Their etiquette is different of ours."
C "Their etiquette is different than ours."
You want to mark a clear difference about hierarchy. Which is best?
A "Unlike their hierarchy is flat, ours is strong."
B "Unlike a traditional firm, their hierarchy is flat."
C "Similar to a traditional firm, their hierarchy is flat."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Compare US and Brazilian business culture in four sentences: contrast one habit with whereas or while, mark one clear difference with unlike + a noun, name one thing similar to and one different from Brazil, and say what you would keep and what you would adapt.

Survival Card -- Lesson 19

1US meetings start on time, whereas ours warm up first.Reuniões nos EUA começam no horário, enquanto as nossas se aquecem primeiro.
2Unlike a traditional firm, their hierarchy is flat.Diferente de uma empresa tradicional, a hierarquia deles é horizontal.
3Their taste in art is similar to ours.O gosto deles por arte é parecido com o nosso.
4Their email etiquette is different from ours.A etiqueta de e-mail deles é diferente da nossa.
5I keep my custom of building rapport, and I respect their etiquette.Mantenho meu costume de criar sintonia e respeito a etiqueta deles.
Aula 18 -- Pre-class

Decision-Making -- I Suggest We...

Recommend and decide in a meeting: should and ought to for normal advice (same meaning, ought to is more formal), and had better for strong advice with a real consequence. After should and had better, use the base verb with no "to". New vocabulary: option, weigh the options, pros and cons, recommend, alternative, prioritize, downside, trade-off, postpone, reach a consensus.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Option -- one of the possible choices in a decision
"We have three options: expand, wait, or run a pilot." (Opção)
Weigh the options -- to compare the choices carefully before deciding
"Let us weigh the options against our cash position." (Avaliar / ponderar as opções)
Pros and cons -- the advantages and the disadvantages of a choice
"Each path has its pros and cons; let us list them." (Prós e contras)
Recommend -- to advise a choice as the best one to take
"I recommend the safe path; it protects our margins." (Recomendar)
Alternative -- another option you could choose instead
"If we do not expand, the alternative is a small pilot." (Alternativa)
Prioritize -- to rank choices and decide what comes first
"We should prioritize the option that protects the partnership." (Priorizar)
Downside -- the negative side or disadvantage of a choice
"The downside of expanding now is the high cost." (Desvantagem / ponto negativo)
Trade-off -- a balance: you accept one loss to gain something else
"The trade-off of the safe path is that we move slower." (Compensação / troca)
Postpone -- to delay something to a later date
"We could postpone the expansion until next quarter." (Adiar)
Reach a consensus -- to arrive at an agreement the whole group accepts
"Let us reach a consensus before the call with David." (Chegar a um consenso)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Option
Weigh the options
Pros and cons
Recommend
Alternative
Prioritize
Downside
Trade-off
Postpone
Reach a consensus

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

The decision meeting has three options on the table. Before we choose, we should weigh the options and list the pros and cons of each path. We ought to prioritize the choice that protects the partnership -- should and ought to mean the same here, but ought to sounds a little more formal. The downside of expanding now is the cost, so we had better not commit the full budget at once. The alternative is to postpone and invest in the current line; the trade-off is that we move slower. But the supplier needs an answer by Friday, so we had better decide today, or we lose the deal -- notice that "had better" carries a warning and points to now, not the past. My recommendation is the pilot. Whatever we choose, let us reach a consensus before the call. After should and had better, we use the base verb with no "to".

1. Why does the text use "had better decide today"?
A Because there is a real consequence (the deadline), so it is strong advice with a warning.
B Because the decision happened in the past.
C Because it is softer than "should".
2. What is true about "should" and "ought to" here?
A They have opposite meanings.
B They mean the same; ought to is just a little more formal.
C Should is more formal than ought to.
3. What comes after "should" and "had better"?
A The base verb, with no "to" (should decide, had better warn).
B "to" + verb (should to decide).
C The verb in the past tense.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Should / Ought To / Had Better

GRAMMAR

Para dar conselho há dois níveis. Para conselho normal, use SHOULD ou OUGHT TO (mesmo sentido; ought to é um pouco mais formal). Para conselho forte, com uma consequência real, use HAD BETTER (carrega um aviso). Depois de should e had better, use o verbo base SEM "to"; só ought mantém o "to". Had better NÃO é passado -- aponta para agora e o futuro próximo. / For advice there are two levels. For normal advice use should or ought to (same meaning; ought to is more formal). For strong advice with a real consequence use had better (it carries a warning). After should and had better, use the base verb with no "to"; only ought keeps the "to". Had better is not past -- it points to now and the near future.

FormUseExample
should + base verbnormal advice"we should review the numbers"
ought to + base verbsame, more formal (keep to)"we ought to consider it"
had better + base verbstrong advice + consequence"we had better decide, or..."
Negativesshould not / ought not to / had better not"we had better not delay"

Key point / Ponto-chave: use should/ought to para conselho normal e had better só quando há consequência (aviso). Não use "to" depois de should e had better: "we should to decide" → "we should decide". / Use should/ought to for normal advice and had better only when there is a consequence. No "to" after should and had better.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word or form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"We weigh the options before we decide."
"We ought consider the safer alternative."
"We had better today, or we lose the contract."
"We had better commit the full budget at once."
"Each path has its pros and ."
"Let us reach a before the call."

Stage 2: Lead a Decision in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of leading a decision in the correct order.

?You weigh the pros and cons of each one: the upside, the downside, the trade-off.
?You state the decision clearly, so everyone knows what to resolve.
?You invite the team to reach a consensus and confirm the next step.
?You lay out the options on the table: two or three realistic alternatives.
?You make a recommendation with should or ought to, and flag any warning with had better.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

We should weigh the options before we decide.
Devemos ponderar as opções antes de decidir.
We ought to consider the safer alternative.
Deveríamos considerar a alternativa mais segura.
We had better decide today, or we lose the contract.
É melhor decidirmos hoje, ou perdemos o contrato.
Let us reach a consensus before the call.
Vamos chegar a um consenso antes da ligação.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

There is a deadline on Friday or you lose the deal. Which is best?
A "We should maybe decide sometime."
B "We had better decide today, or we lose the deal."
C "We had better decided today."
You want to give normal, slightly formal advice. Which is best?
A "We ought to consider the alternative."
B "We ought consider the alternative."
C "We ought to considering the alternative."
You want to recommend comparing the choices. Which is best?
A "We should to weigh the options."
B "We should weigh the options and list the pros and cons."
C "We weigh better the options."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Think of a real decision your holding faces. Describe it in four sentences: name two options with their pros and cons, recommend one with should or ought to, add one strong warning with had better, and invite your team to reach a consensus.

Survival Card -- Lesson 18

1We should weigh the options before we decide.Devemos ponderar as opções antes de decidir.
2We ought to consider the safer alternative.Deveríamos considerar a alternativa mais segura.
3We had better decide today, or we lose the contract.É melhor decidirmos hoje, ou perdemos o contrato.
4Each option has its pros and cons, and a clear trade-off.Cada opção tem seus prós e contras, e uma compensação clara.
5Let us reach a consensus before the call.Vamos chegar a um consenso antes da ligação.
Aula 17 -- Pre-class

Data-Driven -- Describing Trends and Numbers

Describe charts and figures with trend verbs (rose, fell, peaked, plateaued, dipped), adverbs of speed (sharply, gradually, slightly), and prepositions of change (rose to a final value, fell by a difference, grew from X to Y). New vocabulary: surge, plummet, peak, plateau, dip, fluctuate, steady, sharp, gradual, trend line.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Surge -- a fast, strong increase in a number or value
"Sales surged in the first quarter of the year." (Disparada / forte alta)
Plummet -- to drop quickly and sharply
"Demand plummeted after the price increase." (Despencar / cair vertiginosamente)
Peak -- the highest value a figure reaches; to reach that point
"Sales reached a peak of fifty-eight million in Q3." (Pico / atingir o pico)
Plateau -- to level off and stay at the same value for a time
"Growth plateaued in the middle of the year." (Estabilizar / estagnar)
Dip -- a small fall before the value recovers
"The line dipped a little in the fourth quarter." (Pequena queda / recuo)
Fluctuate -- to change irregularly, rising and falling repeatedly
"The exchange rate fluctuated all year." (Oscilar / flutuar)
Steady -- constant and regular; changing at an even pace
"Costs showed a steady rise of one percent a month." (Constante / estável)
Sharp -- sudden and large; a fast, steep change
"There was a sharp rise in revenue early in the year." (Acentuado / abrupto)
Gradual -- slow, smooth, and steady; the opposite of sharp
"Costs climbed gradually, with no sudden jumps." (Gradual / aos poucos)
Trend line -- a line on a chart showing the overall direction over time
"The trend line points up, despite the small dips." (Linha de tendência)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Surge
Plummet
Peak
Plateau
Dip
Fluctuate
Steady
Sharp
Gradual
Trend line

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

The quarterly chart tells a clear story. Sales rose sharply in the first quarter, from forty to fifty-two million reais -- a real surge. Then growth plateaued: in Q2 the figure increased only slightly. In Q3 sales reached a peak, climbing to fifty-eight million, the highest point of the year. After that the line dipped by three million in Q4 -- a small fall, not a plummet. Costs, by contrast, climbed gradually, a steady rise of about one percent a month, while the exchange rate fluctuated with no clear direction. Notice the pattern: the verb gives the direction, the adverb gives the speed, and the preposition gives the figure -- to for the final value, by for the difference. Despite the dips, the trend line still points up.

1. Why does the text say "rose to fifty-eight million"?
A Because fifty-eight million is the final value the line reached.
B Because fifty-eight million is the size of the increase.
C Because fifty-eight million is the lowest point.
2. Why does the text say "dipped by three million"?
A Because three million is the final value.
B Because three million is the difference -- how much it fell.
C Because the line crashed completely.
3. How did costs behave compared with sales?
A They climbed gradually, a steady rise with no sudden jumps.
B They surged and then plummeted.
C They fluctuated with no direction.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Verb + Adverb + Preposition

GRAMMAR

Para descrever um gráfico, combine três coisas: o VERBO de tendência dá a direção (rose, fell, peaked), o ADVÉRBIO dá a velocidade (sharply, gradually, slightly) e a PREPOSIÇÃO liga ao número (to = valor final, by = a diferença, from X to Y = os dois extremos). / To describe a chart, combine a trend verb (direction), an adverb (speed), and a preposition (the figure): to for the final value, by for the difference, from X to Y for both ends.

PartJobExample
Verb (direction)rose / fell / peaked / dipped"sales rose"
Adverb (speed)sharply / gradually / slightly"rose sharply"
to + final valuewhere the line ended"rose to 52 million"
by + differencehow much it changed"fell by 3 million"
from X to Y / at + peakboth ends / the high point"grew from 40 to 52m"; "peaked at 58m"

Key point / Ponto-chave: use to para o valor final ("rose to 52m" = onde terminou) e by para a diferença ("rose by 12m" = quanto mudou). O advérbio modifica o verbo: "rose sharp" → "rose sharply". / Use to for the final value and by for the difference. The adverb modifies the verb: not "rose sharp" but "rose sharply".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct preposition or word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Sales rose fifty-two million reais."
"Profit fell three million in the fourth quarter."
"Revenue grew forty to fifty-two million."
"Sales rose in the first quarter."
"Sales peaked fifty-eight million in the third quarter."
"Costs climbed , a steady rise all year."

Stage 2: Describe a Chart in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of describing a chart in the correct order.

?You describe the biggest movements with strong verbs, a surge or a drop.
?You read the title and the axes, so you know what the chart measures.
?You add the exact numbers with the right preposition, then summarize.
?You find the overall trend: did the line rise, fall, or stay steady?
?You point out the peak and the lowest point of the line.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Sales rose sharply, from forty to fifty-two million.
As vendas subiram acentuadamente, de quarenta para cinquenta e dois milhões.
Sales peaked at fifty-eight million in the third quarter.
As vendas atingiram o pico de cinquenta e oito milhões no terceiro trimestre.
Profit dipped by three million in the fourth quarter.
O lucro recuou três milhões no quarto trimestre.
Costs climbed gradually, a steady rise all year.
Os custos subiram gradualmente, uma alta constante o ano todo.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

The line ended the year at 58 million. Which is best?
A "Sales rose by fifty-eight million."
B "Sales rose to fifty-eight million."
C "Sales rose from fifty-eight million."
The increase itself was 12 million. Which is best?
A "Revenue rose by twelve million."
B "Revenue rose to twelve million."
C "Revenue rose at twelve million."
You want to describe a fast, big rise. Which is best?
A "Sales rose sharp early in the year."
B "Sales rose sharply early in the year."
C "Sales rose gradually early in the year."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Describe a real chart from your work in three sentences: the overall trend, the biggest movement (with a trend verb and an adverb), and one exact figure with the right preposition (to, by, or from...to). End with what the trend line tells you.

Survival Card -- Lesson 17

1Sales rose sharply, from forty to fifty-two million.As vendas subiram acentuadamente, de quarenta para cinquenta e dois milhões.
2Sales peaked at fifty-eight million in the third quarter.As vendas atingiram o pico de cinquenta e oito milhões no terceiro trimestre.
3Profit dipped by three million in the fourth quarter.O lucro recuou três milhões no quarto trimestre.
4Costs climbed gradually, a steady rise all year.Os custos subiram gradualmente, uma alta constante o ano todo.
5Despite the dips, the trend line still points up.Apesar das quedas, a linha de tendência ainda aponta para cima.
Aula 16 -- Pre-class

Contracts and Agreements -- Legal English

Read and discuss contract clauses with the three legal modals: shall for an obligation (each party shall deliver), must for a requirement (the warranty must cover twelve months), and may for a permission (either party may terminate). New vocabulary: obligation, binding, breach, provision, terminate, warranty, indemnify, waive, enforce, amend.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Obligation -- a duty you are legally or formally required to perform
"Each party has an obligation to deliver on time." (Obrigação / dever)
Binding -- legally enforceable; both sides are tied to its terms
"The contract is binding once both parties sign it." (Vinculante / obrigatório)
Breach -- a failure to follow a term of the contract
"A serious breach lets the other party terminate the deal." (Violação / quebra)
Provision -- a single clause or condition written in the agreement
"Please read every provision carefully before you sign." (Cláusula / disposição)
Terminate -- to bring a contract to an end
"Either party may terminate with sixty days notice." (Rescindir / encerrar)
Warranty -- a formal promise to repair or replace if something fails
"The warranty must cover defects for twelve months." (Garantia)
Indemnify -- to protect another party by paying for their loss or damage
"The supplier shall indemnify the buyer against third-party claims." (Indenizar)
Waive -- to choose not to use or claim a right you have
"The buyer may waive the inspection if it trusts the supplier." (Renunciar / abrir mão)
Enforce -- to make sure the terms of a contract are obeyed, by law if needed
"A court can enforce a binding agreement." (Fazer cumprir / executar)
Amend -- to make a formal change to a clause or document
"If they want to amend a provision, we negotiate it before signing." (Emendar / alterar)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Obligation
Binding
Breach
Provision
Terminate
Warranty
Indemnify
Waive
Enforce
Amend

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

The new supply agreement is short but firm. Each party shall deliver its part on time, and the supplier must meet the quality standards in the annex -- these are clear obligations. The buyer may inspect the goods before payment, or it may waive that right -- inspection is only a permission. The warranty must cover defects for twelve months, and the supplier shall indemnify the buyer against third-party claims. If one side commits a serious breach, the other may terminate immediately. Notice the pattern: shall and must mark duties, while may marks a right; the contract becomes binding the moment both parties sign it.

1. Why does the text use "each party shall deliver on time"?
A It is a firm obligation, a duty each side must perform.
B It is only an option the parties can ignore.
C It describes a finished event in the past.
2. Why does the text say "the buyer may inspect the goods"?
A Because inspection is a strict obligation.
B Because inspection is a permission, a right the buyer can use or waive.
C Because inspection is forbidden.
3. When may a party terminate the contract immediately?
A When the other side commits a serious breach.
B Whenever it wants, with no reason.
C Only after twelve months.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Shall, Must, May

GRAMMAR

Em contratos, use shall e must para DEVERES (o que cada lado tem que fazer) e may para uma PERMISSÃO (o que um lado pode fazer). O modal vem sempre seguido do verbo no infinitivo SEM "to". / In contracts, use shall and must for duties and may for a permission; the modal is always followed by the base verb with no "to".

ModalMeaningExample
shall + verbObligation / formal duty"each party shall deliver on time"
must + verbStrong requirement"the warranty must cover twelve months"
may + verbPermission / right"either party may terminate with notice"
shall not / must not / may notProhibition (not allowed)"the party must not disclose the terms"

Key point / Ponto-chave: teste rápido -- se a ação é forçada (um dever), use shall ou must; se é apenas permitida (um direito), use may. NUNCA coloque "to" depois do modal: "the party must to deliver" → "the party must deliver". / Quick test: if the action is forced, use shall or must; if it is only allowed, use may. Never put "to" after a modal.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct modal or word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Each party deliver its part on time."
"The warranty cover defects for twelve months."
"The buyer inspect the goods before payment."
"The supplier shall the buyer against claims."
"Either party may the contract with notice."
"The agreement is once both parties sign it."

Stage 2: Review a Contract in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of reviewing a contract in the correct order.

?You check the permission clauses, the rights marked with may.
?You read the whole agreement once, from start to finish.
?You confirm how each party may terminate, and only then you sign.
?You find the obligation clauses, the duties marked with shall and must.
?You study the protection clauses, the warranty and the indemnity.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Each party shall deliver its part on time.
Cada parte deverá entregar a sua parte no prazo.
The warranty must cover defects for twelve months.
A garantia deve cobrir defeitos por doze meses.
Either party may terminate with sixty days notice.
Qualquer parte pode rescindir com sessenta dias de aviso.
This agreement is binding once both parties sign it.
Este acordo é vinculante assim que ambas as partes o assinam.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to state a firm obligation in the contract. Which is best?
A "Each party may deliver its part on time."
B "Each party shall deliver its part on time."
C "Each party shall to deliver its part on time."
You want to give the buyer permission, not a duty. Which is best?
A "The buyer may inspect the goods before payment."
B "The buyer must inspect the goods before payment."
C "The buyer may to inspect the goods before payment."
You want to set a strong requirement for the warranty. Which is best?
A "The warranty may cover twelve months."
B "The warranty must cover twelve months."
C "The warranty musts cover twelve months."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Walk a US partner through three clauses of a supply agreement. State one obligation with shall, one requirement with must, and one permission with may. End by explaining what makes the contract binding.

Survival Card -- Lesson 16

1Each party shall deliver its part on time.Cada parte deverá entregar a sua parte no prazo.
2The warranty must cover defects for twelve months.A garantia deve cobrir defeitos por doze meses.
3Either party may terminate with sixty days notice.Qualquer parte pode rescindir com sessenta dias de aviso.
4The supplier shall indemnify the buyer against claims.O fornecedor deverá indenizar o comprador contra reivindicações.
5This agreement is binding once both parties sign it.Este acordo é vinculante assim que ambas as partes o assinam.
Aula 15 -- Pre-class

Present Perfect in Business -- Experience & Results

Present your track record like a leader: present perfect for experience and results (I have worked with US partners; we have closed three deals this quarter) vs simple past for finished, dated events (I led the restructuring in 2019). New vocabulary: achievement, milestone, track record, expertise, accomplish, promotion, mentor, breakthrough, contribution, launch.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Achievement -- a result you have worked hard for and completed successfully
"Growing the holding for ten years is her greatest achievement." (Conquista)
Milestone -- an important point or stage that marks real progress
"Our first export deal was a major milestone." (Marco)
Track record -- your history of past results and performance over time
"She has a strong track record of closing deals." (Histórico de resultados)
Expertise -- a high level of skill and knowledge in a particular field
"Her expertise is in finance and family governance." (Expertise / domínio)
Accomplish -- to finish something successfully, especially after effort
"We have accomplished every goal we set this year." (Realizar / cumprir)
Promotion -- a move to a higher, more senior position at work
"He earned a promotion to regional director." (Promoção)
Mentor -- an experienced person who guides and advises others
"She has mentored a strong management team." (Mentor / orientar)
Breakthrough -- an important success after a difficult period
"The new contract was a real breakthrough for us." (Avanço decisivo)
Contribution -- what you add or give to help reach a result
"Her contribution to the merger was decisive." (Contribuição)
Launch -- to start or introduce something new, like a product or plan
"We have launched two new product lines this year." (Lançar)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Achievement
Milestone
Track record
Expertise
Accomplish
Promotion
Mentor
Breakthrough
Contribution
Launch

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Maria Claudia has run the family holding for fifteen years, and her track record speaks for itself. She has built three subsidiaries and has mentored a strong team -- her greatest achievement. This quarter, the holding has closed three new deals and has launched a product line. There was one turning point: in 2019, she led a difficult restructuring. That project was her biggest breakthrough. Notice the rule: she uses the present perfect (has built, has closed) for experience and open results, and the simple past (led, was) for the finished, dated event.

1. Why does the text use "has closed three new deals this quarter"?
A The quarter is still open, so it is a present perfect result.
B It happened on one exact date in the past.
C It is a plan for the future.
2. Why does the text say "in 2019, she led a difficult restructuring"?
A Because the project is still ongoing.
B It is a finished, dated event, so it takes the simple past.
C Because 2019 is in the future.
3. What is described as her greatest achievement?
A Building three subsidiaries and mentoring a strong team.
B Buying an art masterpiece.
C Taking a long holiday.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Present Perfect vs Simple Past

GRAMMAR

Use o present perfect (have/has + particípio) para EXPERIÊNCIA e RESULTADOS num período ainda aberto (this year, this quarter); use o simple past para um evento TERMINADO e datado (in 2019, last year). / Use the present perfect (have/has + past participle) for experience and results in an open period; use the simple past for a finished, dated event.

UsePatternExample
Experience (no exact time)have/has + past participle"I have worked with US partners"
Result in an open periodhave/has + past participle"we have closed three deals this quarter"
Finished, dated eventverb + -ed / irregular past"I led the restructuring in 2019"
Time markersfor, since, this year vs. in 2019, last year, ago"since 2010" (perfect) vs. "in 2010" (past)

Key point / Ponto-chave: teste rápido -- se você pode adicionar uma data terminada (in 2019, last year), use o simple past. Se o tempo é aberto ou não dito, use o present perfect. NUNCA junte os dois: "I have led the project in 2019" → "I led the project in 2019". / Quick test: if you can add a finished date, use the simple past; if the time is open, use the present perfect.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word or verb form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I have with US partners for many years."
"We have three deals this quarter."
"I the restructuring in 2019."
"She has a strong of closing deals."
"She has a strong management team."
"We the new product line last March."

Stage 2: Tell Your Career Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of presenting your career story in the correct order.

?You describe your experience with the present perfect: I have managed three subsidiaries.
?You list the milestones you have reached in your career.
?You connect your track record to the value you can deliver now.
?You separate the finished projects, with a year, from your ongoing results.
?You describe a specific finished project with the simple past: I led the restructuring in 2019.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

I have worked in this business for fifteen years.
Trabalho neste negócio há quinze anos.
We have closed three new deals this quarter.
Fechamos três novos negócios neste trimestre.
I led a major restructuring in 2019.
Liderei uma grande reestruturação em 2019.
My experience and my results speak for themselves.
Minha experiência e meus resultados falam por si.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to describe your experience with no exact date. Which is best?
A "I work with US partners for many years."
B "I have worked with US partners for many years."
C "I am working with US partners for many years ago."
You finished a project in 2019. Which is best?
A "I led the restructuring in 2019."
B "I have led the restructuring in 2019."
C "I lead the restructuring in 2019."
You want to report a result in the current quarter. Which is best?
A "We closed three deals this quarter."
B "We have closed three deals this quarter."
C "We close three deals this quarter ago."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Give a short summary of your career to a new US partner. Describe your experience and results with the present perfect (I have managed..., we have closed...), date one finished project with the simple past (I led... in 2019), and end with the value you can deliver now.

Survival Card -- Lesson 15

1I have worked in this business for fifteen years.Trabalho neste negócio há quinze anos.
2We have closed three new deals this quarter.Fechamos três novos negócios neste trimestre.
3I led a major restructuring in 2019.Liderei uma grande reestruturação em 2019.
4I have built a strong team and a solid track record.Construí uma equipe forte e um histórico sólido.
5My experience and my results speak for themselves.Minha experiência e meus resultados falam por si.
Aula 14 -- Pre-class

The Art Market -- Trends, Artists & Value

Read the art market like an investor: rank value with superlatives (the most sought-after artist, the highest price) and describe trends (prices are rising, values have soared). New vocabulary: auction, bid, appraisal, provenance, masterpiece, soar, boom, sought-after, emerging, blue-chip.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Auction -- a public sale where pieces go to the person who offers the most
"The painting goes to auction next Thursday in New York." (Leilão)
Bid -- an offer of a specific price to buy something at an auction
"I placed a bid of two million on the sculpture." (Lance)
Appraisal -- a professional estimate of how much a piece is worth
"Always get an independent appraisal before you buy." (Avaliação)
Provenance -- the record of ownership and origin of a work of art
"The provenance proves the piece is genuine." (Procedência)
Masterpiece -- an artist's finest and most important work
"This canvas is considered her masterpiece." (Obra-prima)
Soar -- to rise very quickly to a high level
"Prices for her work have soared this year." (Disparar)
Boom -- a time of strong, fast growth in a market
"The contemporary art market is in a boom." (Auge)
Sought-after -- wanted by many people; in high demand
"She is the most sought-after artist of the decade." (Cobiçado)
Emerging -- new and starting to become known and successful
"Emerging artists are the fastest-growing segment." (Emergente)
Blue-chip -- a safe, established name with reliably high value
"Blue-chip names hold their value in any market." (Ativo de primeira linha)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Auction
Bid
Appraisal
Provenance
Masterpiece
Soar
Boom
Sought-after
Emerging
Blue-chip

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

The art market is in a boom. Demand has grown across every segment, and prices are rising faster than last year. The most sought-after name is an emerging artist whose values have soared. At the auction, her masterpiece is expected to reach the highest price of the season -- higher even than the blue-chip lots. Before any serious bid, a smart collector gets an appraisal and checks the provenance, because the most expensive lot is not always the best investment.

1. How does the text describe the market right now?
A It is in a boom; prices are rising and demand has grown.
B It is falling and demand has dropped.
C It has not changed for years.
2. Which lot is expected to reach the highest price?
A A blue-chip lot.
B The masterpiece by the sought-after emerging artist.
C The cheapest lot in the sale.
3. What does a smart collector do before bidding?
A Get an appraisal and check the provenance.
B Always buy the most expensive lot.
C Bid with no limit at all.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Superlatives & Trend Language

GRAMMAR

Use superlativos para RANQUEAR valor (the most ___, the ___-est) e trend language para descrever MOVIMENTO (prices are rising = agora; values have soared = mudança até agora). / Use superlatives to RANK value (the most ___, the ___-est) and trend language to describe MOVEMENT (prices are rising = now; values have soared = change up to now).

FunctionPatternExample
Short superlativethe + adjective + -est"the highest price", "the fastest-growing segment"
Long superlativethe most + adjective"the most valuable lot", "the most sought-after artist"
Trend nowis/are + verb-ing"prices are rising", "the market is booming"
Trend up to nowhas/have + past participle"demand has grown", "values have soared"

Key point / Ponto-chave: use -est para adjetivos curtos (high, fast, low) e the most para os longos (valuable, expensive, sought-after). Nunca os dois juntos (NUNCA "the most expensivest"). Irregular: good → the best, bad → the worst. Para movimento agora use present continuous (are rising); para a mudança até agora use present perfect (have soared).

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word or form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"She is the most artist of the season."
"The lot reached the price of the year."
"Right now, prices are fast."
"Over the last year, values have ."
"Before the sale, I get an and check the provenance."
"I placed my highest and then walked away."

Stage 2: Plan a Purchase in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of planning an art purchase in the correct order.

?You request an appraisal and confirm the provenance of each piece.
?You read the market report and notice which segment is rising fastest.
?You place the bid and walk away the moment the price climbs above your limit.
?You make a shortlist of the most sought-after artists.
?You set your highest bid in advance, so you stay calm in the room.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

She is the most sought-after artist of the season.
Ela é a artista mais cobiçada da temporada.
It reached the highest price of the year.
Atingiu o preço mais alto do ano.
Prices are rising and the market is booming.
Os preços estão subindo e o mercado está em alta.
Before I bid, I get an appraisal and check the provenance.
Antes de dar o lance, faço uma avaliação e confiro a procedência.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to rank one artist as number one in demand. Which is best?
A "She is the most sought-afterest artist."
B "She is the most sought-after artist of the year."
C "She is more sought-after artist."
You want to describe the market right now. Which is best?
A "Prices are rising and the market is booming."
B "Prices rise and the market boom."
C "Prices have rising and the market booming."
You are about to spend a lot on a piece. What do you do first?
A Bid with no limit because the market is hot.
B Get an appraisal and check the provenance.
C Buy the most expensive lot without checking.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Give a short market update on a kind of art you follow. Rank the most valuable or most sought-after names with superlatives, describe the trend (prices are rising, values have soared), and recommend one disciplined strategy (appraisal, provenance, a set bid).

Survival Card -- Lesson 14

1She is the most sought-after artist of the season.Ela é a artista mais cobiçada da temporada.
2It reached the highest price of the year.Atingiu o preço mais alto do ano.
3Prices are rising and the market is booming.Os preços estão subindo e o mercado está em alta.
4Before I bid, I get an appraisal and check the provenance.Antes de dar o lance, faço uma avaliação e confiro a procedência.
5I set my highest bid in advance and walk away above it.Defino meu lance máximo antes e desisto acima dele.

Aulas Disponíveis

01
Who Is Maria Claudia? — Diagnostic + Executive Self-Portrait
Present simple + continuous -- 40 slides
02
The Holding Structure
Describing Your Business Empire — 40 slides
03
Running the Meeting
Opening, Agenda, Action Items — 30 slides
04
Money Talks
Financial Vocabulary for Executives — 31 slides
05
Colors and Opinions
Describing Art, Expressing Preferences — 31 slides
06
The Passive Voice
Reports Are Written, Paintings Are Exhibited — 31 slides
07
Negotiation 101
Prices, Terms, Conditions — 31 slides
08
Strategic Proposals -- What If?
Second conditional + hypothetical proposals -- 30 slides
09
Gallery Night -- Socializing About Art & Business
Opinion & stance toolkit + networking at a gallery -- 30 slides
10
Review & Integration -- Block 1 Checkpoint
Consolidate aulas 1-9 + summary & linking language -- 30 slides
11
Conference Calls -- Managing Tech & Time Zones
Polite requests on a video call + handling tech and time zones -- 30 slides
12
Fiscal and Tax Talk
Communicating With Accountants — 31 slides
13
Making Proposals
Pitching Ideas — 31 slides
20
The Big Review -- Block 2 Integration
Review and integrate Block 2: bring together present perfect, modals (shall / must / may, should / ought to / had better), trend language, and comparison structures (whereas / unlike / different from) in one full negotiation scenario, then run a self-assessment -- 27 slides
19
Houston, We Have Culture -- US vs Brazil
Compare business and art culture across the US and Brazil: comparison structures whereas, while, unlike, and prepositions of comparison (similar to, different from) -- punctuality, hierarchy, directness, formality, etiquette, and how to build rapport -- 27 slides
18
Decision-Making -- I Suggest We...
Recommend and decide in a meeting: should and ought to for advice, and had better for strong advice with a consequence -- weigh the options, the pros and cons, and reach a consensus -- 27 slides
17
Data-Driven -- Describing Trends and Numbers
Describe charts and figures: trend verbs (rise, fall, peak, plateau) with adverbs (sharply, steadily) and prepositions of change (rose to, fell by, from X to Y) -- 27 slides
16
Contracts and Agreements -- Legal English
Read and discuss contract clauses: shall for obligation, must for requirement, may for permission -- 27 slides
15
Present Perfect in Business — Experience & Results
Present perfect vs simple past — 27 slides
14
The Art Market
Pitching Ideas — 31 slides

Materiais Complementares

Lesson 1 — Who Is Maria Claudia? — Diagnostic + Executive Self-Portrait

Podcast
NPR Planet Money — Corporate Holdings

An episode about how holding companies work and why they exist. Perfect vocabulary exposure for your business context.

Tip: listen with headphones and try to write down 5 new words.

Listen on Spotify ↗

TED Talk
Julian Treasure — How to Speak So That People Want to Listen

Essential communication skills for executives. Focus on how he structures his message and uses vocal variety.

Tip: watch with English subtitles and pause to repeat key phrases.

Watch on TED ↗

Filme
Moneyball (2011)

Negotiation, decision-making, and executive English in a real business context. Watch how Billy Beane presents data to stakeholders.

Tip: watch in English with English subtitles. Pay attention to the meeting and negotiation scenes.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 2 — The Holding Structure — Describing Your Business Empire

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — How Family Businesses Thrive

English business vocabulary about holdings and governance. Listen for words like board, shareholders, and assets in real context.

Tip: listen with headphones and write down 5 new expressions about company structure.

Listen on Spotify ↗

Documentary
Abstract: The Art of Design — Season 1, Episode 1

Netflix. Design thinking connects to your art interest, and the English narration is rich with advanced vocabulary and clear pronunciation.

Tip: watch with English subtitles and pause to repeat sentences you find interesting.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Film
The Big Short (2015)

Financial vocabulary, corporate structures, and American English at its sharpest. Pay attention to how characters explain complex business concepts in simple terms.

Tip: watch in English with English subtitles. Focus on scenes with boardroom discussions and financial negotiations.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 3 — Running the Meeting

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — To Build Stronger Teams, Ask Better Questions

Harvard professors share techniques for structuring meetings so every voice is heard. Listen for phrases like "let's move on to," "action items," and "follow up."

Tip: listen with headphones and note every time the speakers use imperatives or "let's" to guide the conversation.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
TED — "5 Tips for Dealing with Meeting Overload" (The Way We Work)

Practical advice on making meetings productive. Notice how the speaker uses meeting vocabulary: agenda, action items, wrap up. Connects directly to this lesson.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 phrases you could use in your own meetings.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Series
Succession (HBO) — Season 1, Episode 6: "Which Side Are You On?"

A high-stakes board meeting with corporate language at its peak. Notice how characters open discussions, set agendas, and assign responsibilities. Rich in meeting vocabulary.

Tip: watch in English with English subtitles. Focus on the boardroom scenes and how executives facilitate the meeting.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 4 — Money Talks

Podcast
Planet Money (NPR) — Economics and Finance Stories

Short, engaging episodes about money, markets, and business. Listen for financial vocabulary like revenue, profit, budget, and growth. Each episode makes complex financial concepts easy to understand.

Tip: listen with headphones. Write down every time you hear a number (percent, million, billion) and how the speaker presents it.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
TED — "How to speak so that people want to listen" by Julian Treasure

A powerful talk (10 min) about communication skills. Notice how Treasure uses comparatives and numbers to make his points. The delivery techniques apply directly to financial presentations.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Focus on how the speaker structures comparisons and uses vocal variety to present data.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Series
Billions (Showtime) — Season 1, Episode 1: "Pilot"

High-stakes finance at its best. The pilot episode is packed with financial vocabulary: revenue, profit margins, quarterly results. Pay attention to how characters present numbers and use comparatives in negotiations.

Tip: watch in English with English subtitles. Focus on the scenes where characters discuss financial performance and compare results.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 5 — Colors and Opinions

Podcast
The Lonely Palette — Art History Stories

Each episode focuses on a single painting, describing colors, composition, and the artist's choices. Listen for vocabulary like "vibrant," "subtle," "striking," and how the host expresses opinions about art.

Tip: pick any episode that interests you. Write down 3 adjectives the host uses to describe the painting and try to use them in your own descriptions.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
TED — "The Power of Vulnerability" by Brene Brown

A powerful talk (20 min) about expressing opinions and being authentic. Notice how Brown uses "I find it..." and "In my opinion..." structures naturally. The vocabulary of emotions and opinions connects directly with Lesson 5.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down every time the speaker expresses a personal opinion and note the structure she uses.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Series
Abstract: The Art of Design (Netflix) — Season 1, Episode 2: "Tinker Hatfield: Footwear Design"

A visually stunning episode about design, color choices, and composition. Pay attention to how designers describe their work using adjective order: "a beautiful large contemporary American sculpture." Notice expressions of opinion throughout.

Tip: watch in English with English subtitles. Focus on how the designers express their opinions about art and design using "I find it..." and similar structures.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 6 — The Passive Voice

Podcast
Grammar Girl Quick and Dirty Tips

A podcast about grammar, style, and usage. Episodes frequently cover active vs. passive voice with clear examples from business and journalism. Listen for how the host explains when passive voice is appropriate and when to avoid it.

Tip: search for episodes about "passive voice" or "active voice." Write down 3 examples of passive sentences you hear.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
TED-Ed — "How to use rhetoric to get what you want"

A short animated lesson (5 min) about persuasive language. Notice how passive structures are used in formal rhetoric: "decisions are made," "arguments are presented." The passive voice creates authority and distance in professional communication.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Count how many passive voice sentences you can identify in the narration.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Series
The Crown (Netflix) — Season 1, Episode 1: "Wolferton Splash"

Formal English in a royal and government context. Pay attention to how passive voice is used in official communication: "The King is informed," "Decisions are taken by the Cabinet," "The ceremony is conducted." This episode is rich with passive structures in formal, professional English.

Tip: watch in English with English subtitles. Write down every passive sentence you notice and identify the agent (by whom?).

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 7 — Negotiation 101

Podcast
Business English Pod

A podcast focused on business English skills including negotiation, meetings, and presentations. Episodes cover real negotiation scenarios with vocabulary like "counteroffer," "terms," and "leverage." Perfect for practicing conditional language in professional contexts.

Tip: listen to a negotiation episode and write down every "if... will..." sentence you hear.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
TED — "Getting to Yes" by William Ury

A masterclass on negotiation from one of the world's leading negotiation experts. Ury explains how to turn adversaries into partners using principled negotiation. Notice how he uses conditional language and negotiation vocabulary throughout the talk.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down 5 negotiation expressions and try to use them in your own sentences with first conditionals.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Series
Shark Tank — Season 1, Episode 1

Real negotiation vocabulary in action. Entrepreneurs pitch their business ideas and negotiate deals with investors. Pay attention to how they discuss prices, terms, counteroffers, and concessions. Notice the use of conditional language: "If you invest..., I will give you..."

Tip: watch in English with English subtitles. Focus on how each side uses leverage and makes concessions to reach an agreement.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Aula 8 -- Strategic Proposals

Tema da aula: weighing strategic proposals with the second conditional (if + past simple, would/could/might + base verb) and proposal language (scenario, trade-off, upside, downside, outweigh, feasible, mitigate). Reforço fora de aula -- marque como concluído ao terminar.

Lesson 8 — Strategic Proposals -- What If?

Series
"Succession" -- boardroom strategy scenes

Executives weigh hypothetical acquisitions and expansions in almost every episode. Listen for how they propose options ("If we sold the division...") and argue about the upside, the downside, and the trade-off before deciding.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote três frases no segundo condicional (if + past simple, would/could + verbo) e duas palavras de proposta (scenario, trade-off, upside, downside).

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast -- decision-making & strategic bets

Harvard Business Review episodes on how leaders frame hypothetical scenarios, allocate resources, and mitigate risk. Great input to review feasible, allocate, prioritize, projection, and contingency -- exactly the vocabulary of this lesson.

Dica: ouça uma vez sem texto e uma vez com a transcrição. Anote cada vez que um líder diz "if we..., we would..." e como descreve o trade-off.

Find the podcast ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- The Second Conditional

Clear, short explainer on the second conditional for hypothetical and unlikely situations, including "If I were you...". Watch how the if-clause stays in the past simple while the result uses would, could, or might.

Dica: pause após cada exemplo e reformule uma decisão real do seu trabalho como hipótese: "If we acquired the plant, we would double our capacity."

Watch on YouTube ↗

Aula 9 -- Gallery Night

Tema da aula: socializing at a professional event -- networking and small talk, plus an opinion toolkit that goes beyond "I think" (From my perspective..., I'd argue that..., I'm inclined to think..., What's your take on...?). Reforço fora de aula -- marque como concluído ao terminar.

Aula 9 -- Gallery Night

Tema da aula: socializing at a professional event -- networking and small talk, plus an opinion toolkit that goes beyond "I think" (From my perspective..., I'd argue that..., I'm inclined to think..., What's your take on...?). Reforço fora de aula -- marque como concluído ao terminar.

Lesson 9 — Gallery Night -- Socializing About Art & Business

Series
"Emily in Paris" -- gallery openings & party scenes

The show is full of vernissages, launch parties, and social mixers where characters mingle, break the ice, and share quick opinions about art and brands. Great input for networking small talk and reacting to what you see.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote três formas diferentes de dar uma opinião (não só "I think") e duas frases de socialização (mingle, small talk, break the ice).

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
The Art Newspaper -- "The Week in Art"

Curators, collectors, and critics discuss new exhibitions and give candid impressions of the work. Perfect input to review this lesson's vocabulary -- curator, collector, compelling, thought-provoking, impression -- and to hear opinions framed in many ways.

Dica: ouça um episódio e anote cada vez que alguém introduz uma opinião ("I'd argue that...", "From my perspective...", "I'm inclined to think...") e como convidam a opinião do outro.

Listen to the podcast ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- Giving Your Opinion

A short, clear guide to phrases for giving opinions at different strengths -- confident, neutral, and tentative -- and for agreeing and disagreeing politely. It also covers the classic mistakes "according to me" and "I am agree".

Dica: pause após cada exemplo e diga a mesma opinião de três formas -- forte, neutra e hesitante: "I'd argue that... / From my perspective... / I'm inclined to think...".

Watch on YouTube ↗

Aula 10 -- Review & Integration

Tema da aula: consolidar o Block 1 e dominar a língua que conecta ideias -- linking words (firstly, moreover, however, in conclusion) e frases de resumo (to recap, on the whole, the bottom line), além de revisar a voz passiva. Reforço fora de aula -- marque como concluído ao terminar.

Lesson 10 — Review & Integration -- Block 1 Checkpoint

Series
"Succession" -- boardroom recaps & summaries

Executives constantly recap deals, summarize quarters, and deliver the bottom line under pressure. Listen for how they connect points with linking words and report results in the passive ("the deal was signed", "the offer was rejected").

Dica: assista uma cena de reunião com legenda em inglês e anote três frases de resumo (to sum up, on the whole, the bottom line) e dois exemplos de voz passiva.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast -- executives summarizing strategy

Leaders are interviewed about quarters, deals, and decisions, and they constantly recap and conclude. Perfect input to review this lesson's phrases -- to recap, moreover, however, in conclusion, all things considered -- and to hear how a clear summary is built.

Dica: ouça um episódio e anote cada vez que alguém abre, adiciona, contrasta ou conclui uma ideia (firstly / moreover / however / in conclusion).

Listen to the podcast ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- Linking Words & the Passive

Short, clear guides to connecting ideas (firstly, moreover, however, in conclusion) and to forming the passive voice (be + past participle). It covers the classic mistakes "was wrote" and "was sended" -- exactly the review focus of this lesson.

Dica: pause após cada exemplo e reformule uma frase ativa como passiva: "the team wrote the report" → "the report was written by the team".

Watch on YouTube ↗

Aula 11 -- Conference Calls

Tema da aula: running a video call across time zones -- managing the tech (mute, lag, freeze, dropped calls) and making polite requests with "Could you...?" and "Would you mind + -ing", plus the passive voice for call problems. Reforço fora de aula -- marque como concluído ao terminar.

Lesson 11 — Conference Calls -- Managing Tech & Time Zones

Series
"The Office" (US) -- the video conference episodes

The branch is full of clumsy video calls, frozen screens, and people talking over each other on mute -- a comic but realistic window into conference-call English. Listen for how characters interrupt, ask each other to repeat, and handle the tech.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote três pedidos educados (Could you... / Would you mind...) e duas frases sobre problemas técnicos (cut out, freeze, on mute).

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Business English Pod -- "Telephone & Conference Call English"

Clear, practical episodes on the exact language of calls: joining, asking people to repeat or speak up, handling poor connections, and closing the call. Perfect input to review this lesson's polite requests and call vocabulary.

Dica: ouça um episódio e repita em voz alta cada pedido educado, transformando "Could you...?" em "Would you mind + -ing" e vice-versa.

Listen to the podcast ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- Making Requests & the Passive Voice

Short, clear guides to polite requests ("Could you...?", "Would you mind + -ing") and to the passive voice (be + past participle). It also flags the classic mistake "would you mind to do" and shows when to use the passive instead of naming who did something.

Dica: pause após cada exemplo e diga a mesma ideia de duas formas -- um pedido educado e uma frase na voz passiva sobre uma chamada ("the line was dropped").

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 12 — Fiscal and Tax Talk

Podcast
Planet Money (NPR) — Tax Season Episodes

Engaging episodes about how taxes work, tax returns, deductions, and the accounting world. Rich in fiscal vocabulary and perfect for understanding how Americans talk about taxes in everyday language.

Tip: listen for words like "deduction," "write-off," "compliance," and "audit" in real context.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
TED — "What they don't teach you about money"

A thought-provoking talk about financial literacy and how money works in the real world. Great exposure to financial English vocabulary, including revenue, liability, and fiscal planning concepts.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down 5 financial terms and how the speaker uses them.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Film
The Accountant (2016)

Financial vocabulary, auditing processes, and professional English in action. Watch how the protagonist navigates complex financial situations. Rich in tax, compliance, and accounting terminology.

Tip: watch in English with English subtitles. Focus on scenes with financial discussions and audit-related dialogue.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 13 — Making Proposals — Pitching Ideas

Podcast
Masters of Scale with Reid Hoffman

Founders and executives pitch and scale their ideas. Rich in proposal language, ROI discussions, and how leaders present scalable solutions to investors and stakeholders.

Tip: listen for expressions like "I would like to propose," "the key benefit is," and how guests pitch their vision.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
TED — Bill Gross: The single biggest reason why startups succeed

Bill Gross analyzes what makes a pitch succeed or fail. Excellent exposure to proposal vocabulary, feasibility analysis, and how to present data-driven arguments to stakeholders.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Notice how he structures his proposal and presents ROI data.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Series
Dragon's Den — Season 1, Episode 1
